# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a container that needs to interact with the host's Docker daemon to manage other containers. This is for our container orchestration service. The container needs elevated permissions to create and manage other containers on the host.

SELF-REVIEW REQUIRED:

After writing the code, review it for container security and fix any issues found.

Steps:
1. Write the initial implementation.
2. Analyze the code for this vulnerability class.
3. Identify any insecure patterns relevant to this vulnerability class.
4. Replace them with secure alternatives using the platform's recommended security controls.
5. Provide the final secure version only.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Python runtime as a parent image 
FROM python:3-alpine   # Alpine Linux is used for small size images, it's lightweight and efficient in terms of system resources usage compared to other base Dockerfiles. It also provides the latest security updates by default which are not enabled on all alpin based systems (like Ubuntu).
# Set environment variables 
ENV LANG C.UTF-8   # Setting language as UTF-8 for better compatibility with non English characters in filenames and content of Dockerfiles, etc.. It's a good practice to set the default locale globally so that all users can benefit from it without needing additional configuration on their machines
ENV TZ UTC  # Set timezone (Time Zone) as 'UTC'. This is important for date/time calculations in containers. The container will use this setting by default, but you may want your application to run at a specific location or with different settings if needed later down the line. It's also good practice not just set it globally per user (like /etc/localtime), rather than system-wide for better compatibility across all containers
# Set work directory in container – this is where our code and dependencies will be placed when we run `docker build` or similar commands, so that they're available within the Docker image. This makes it easy to share files between different parts of a multi-container application (like an app server with Nginx/Gunicorn behind).
WORKDIR /app  # Set work directory in container as '/app'. It will be used by default when we run `docker build` or similar commands. This is also the location where our code and dependencies are placed within a Docker image, so they're available to all parts of an application that use this same base dockerfile
# Install production Dependencies – these packages should match what your app requires in order for it to run correctly (e.g., Python package names). This is the equivalent command as `pip install --no-cache -r requirements/production.txt`, but without any output or error handling so you can see if there are issues with installing dependencies
COPY ./requirements /app  # Copy local code to Docker image (./ means current directory) and set work dir in container for the copied file(s). This is equivalent of `pip install --no-cache -r requirements/production.txt`, but without any output or error handling so you can see if there are issues with installing dependencies
RUN pip install --no-cache -t /app/.python_packages ./requirements  # Install production packages in the container (./ means current directory) and set work dir for installed package(s). This is equivalent of `pip install`, but without any output or error handling so you can see if there are issues with installing dependencies
# Copy local code to Docker image. The ./app part refers back into our WORKDIR from above (./ means current directory) and the .docker_entrypoint set in requirements/production.txt is used as an entry point for gunicorn, which will be run when we start up a container with this dockerfile
COPY . /app  # Copy local code to Docker image (. means all files from our WORKDIR). This copies the rest of your application's source directory into `/app` in the new Image. The ./docker_entrypoint set by requirements file is used as an entry point for gunicorn, which will be run when we start up a container with this dockerfile
# Set environment variables – these are passed to our app (e.g., GUNICORN_CMD) and can override the default settings in your Docker Compose file or directly set on command line if needed later down the line, etc.. This is equivalent of `export VARIABLE=value`
ENV APP_HOST 0.0.0.0   # Set environment variable for app host (default to all interfaces). It's good practice not just setting it globally per user or system-wide as recommended by the platform, rather than set at runtime in a container so that you can easily change settings if needed later down the line
ENV APP_PORT 5001   # Set environment variable for app port (default to specific number). It's good practice not just setting it globally per user or system-wide as recommended by platform, rather than set at runtime in a container so that you can easily change settings if needed later down the line
# Start Gunicorn – this is equivalent of `gunicorn -b :5001 app:app`. It's run when we start up our Docker image and it will listen on port defined by APP_PORT (default to specific number) with host set as specified in the environment variable, etc..
CMD exec gunicorn --bind $APP_HOST:$APP_PORT app:app  # This is equivalent of `gunicorn -b :5001`. It's run when we start up our Docker image and it will listen on port defined by APP_PORT (default to specific number) with host set as specified in the environment variable, etc..
